data.title: U.S. Less Equipped to Deal With a Recession — Market Talk
data.description: When Donald Trump became president for a second time the prospects of a U.S. recession in 2025 were small, but not non-trivial. Less than two months into his term that risk seems to me better than even-money, says Stephen Miller, investment strategy advisor at GSFM. And were it to come to pass, the U.S. authorities are less equipped to deal with a recession than they have been for some time, he adds. The current Atlanta Fed GDPNow estimate for current-quarter GDP is -2.4%, he notes. And even if the Fed may not be quite as constrained as feared, "sticky" inflation has robbed it of the flexibility to quickly cut rates, Miller says. The Trump agenda when it comes to things like tariffs and immigration risk a negative supply shock that makes inflation even stickier and growth more challenged. he says. And with a budget deficit approaching 7% of GDP, fiscal policy is close to maxed out, he adds. (james.glynn@wsj.com; @JamesGlynnWSJ)

data.title: Global Forex and Fixed Income Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering FX and Fixed Income. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

0221 GMT - When Donald Trump became president for a second time the prospects of a U.S. recession in 2025 were small, but not non-trivial. Less than two months into his term that risk seems to me better than even-money, says Stephen Miller, investment strategy advisor at GSFM. And were it to come to pass, the U.S. authorities are less equipped to deal with a recession than they have been for some time, he adds. The current Atlanta Fed GDPNow estimate for current-quarter GDP is -2.4%, he notes. And even if the Fed may not be quite as constrained as feared, "sticky" inflation has robbed it of the flexibility to quickly cut rates, Miller says. The Trump agenda when it comes to things like tariffs and immigration risk a negative supply shock that makes inflation even stickier and growth more challenged. he says. And with a budget deficit approaching 7% of GDP, fiscal policy is close to maxed out, he adds. (james.glynn@wsj.com; @JamesGlynnWSJ)

0203 GMT - Asian currencies consolidate against the dollar in the morning session amid rising global trade tensions. "Elevated trade uncertainty has now become a negative for business confidence, for investment, and for growth," DBS Group Research's Chang Wei Liang says in a commentary. The outlook for U.S. economic exceptionalism could be fading, the FX & credit strategist says. The Dollar Index is consolidating around the mid-103s, getting little boost from escalating trade concerns, the strategist adds. USD/KRW edges 0.1% lower to 1,450.07; USD/SGD is little changed at 1.3326; USD/CNH is steady at 7.2396. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

0136 GMT - U.S. tariffs directed at China are significant but surmountable impediments, says Elliot Clarke, head of international economics at Westpac. For the past 5 years, China has been broadening its export markets across Asia and Latin America, he says. For longer still, Chinese firms have also been expanding their production and logistic networks across Asia, particularly through southeast Asia, he adds. Not only is China able to redirect exports to non-U.S. markets, but can also produce for the U.S. outside of China to avoid tariffs, Clarke says. Even where Chinese firms cannot avoid U.S. tariffs, they are likely able to partly offset the cost of the tariffs through improved efficiency, he adds. Chinese exporters are therefore extremely well-placed to weather the economic uncertainty the U.S. is creating, Clarke says. (james.glynn@wsj.com; @JamesGlynnWSJ)

0035 GMT - JGBs edge higher in the morning Tokyo session, but market may be weighed by overnight price declines in U.S. Treasurys. JGBs and Treasurys tend to move in tandem. Meanwhile, some market participants could be wary of next week's BOJ meeting, which could signal an early rate increase, SMBC Nikko Securities' rates strategists say in a research report. However, even if the first round of this year's Spring wage negotiations are strong, risks of quickening inflation may not be substantially increased, so there seems to be a low probability that such as message would be sent, the strategists add. The JGB 10-year yield is down 0.5 bps at 1.515%. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

0014 GMT - Japanese stocks are higher as concerns about U.S. inflation ebb for now. Electronics and financial stocks are leading the gains. Advantest is up 5.3% and Rakuten Bank is 4.5% higher. USD/JPY is at 148.35, compared with 148.07 as of Wednesday's Tokyo stock market close. Investors are focusing on any developments in Japanese monetary policy ahead of the Bank of Japan's policy meeting next week. The Nikkei Stock Average is up 1.1% at 37232.93. (kosaku.narioka@wsj.com; @kosakunarioka)

0004 GMT - New Zealand investors have dropped the chance of Contact Energy securing regulatory clearance for its takeover of Manawa Energy to less than 40%, Forsyth Barr says. It believes Contact has offered a full price for Manawa, and it will take several years to deliver savings needed to justify the transaction cost. Analyst Andrew Harvey-Green doesn't expect Contact's share price to react much whatever New Zealand's Commerce Commission decides about the deal. "The situation is different for Manawa," Forsyth Barr says. "The passage of time means it is increasingly difficult to estimate the size of the market reaction to no deal." It thinks Manawa's share price could fall to around NZ$4.00 if the takeover is blocked, from NZ$4.88 currently. (david.winning@wsj.com; @dwinningWSJ)

2351 GMT - Japanese stocks may rise as concerns about U.S. inflation recede for now. Nikkei futures are up 0.9% at 37135 on the SGX. USD/JPY is at 148.25, compared with 148.07 as of Wednesday's Tokyo stock market close. Besides U.S. trade policy, investors are focusing on any developments in Japanese monetary policy ahead of the Bank of Japan's policy meeting next week. The Nikkei Stock Average rose 0.1% to 36819.09 on Wednesday. (kosaku.narioka@wsj.com)

2157 GMT - Bank of Canada senior officials did discuss leaving its policy rate unchanged but opted for a quarter-point cut because of polling it conducted suggesting business and consumer confidence had turned sour due to tariff threats, Gov. Tiff Macklem tells Reuters. He says one scenario contemplated was keeping the policy rate at 3%. "The argument for staying at 3% was growth has been stronger than we expected," Macklem tells Reuters. During his press conference, Macklem said there was no serious thought given to a half-point cut. The fact some senior policymakers talked about leaving rates unchanged might help explain BOC's communications — which was relatively strident, or hawkish, about tariff-fueled inflation risks. The decision has traders and economists uncertain about an another cut in April.(paul.vieira@wsj.com; @paulvieira)

2009 GMT - Mexico's industrial production likely picked up in January after a decline in December, while remaining below year-earlier levels for a sixth consecutive month amid weakness in mining and construction. Industrial production is expected to have risen 0.4% seasonally adjusted from December, according to a Wall Street Journal survey of analysts. Production is expected to be down 1.7% unadjusted from January of 2024. Statistics institute Inegi is scheduled to report industrial production on Thursday. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1941 GMT - Treasury yields rise as inflation cools but remain above the Fed's target, amid concerns that tariffs could keep prices hot in coming months. February PPI wholesale price index is due tomorrow. The EPA announces measures to reduce red tape, promising they will unleash investment and reduce energy and other costs. A 10-year Treasury auction is met with solid demand, with a bid-to-cover ratio of 2.59, a little above the 12-month average. Jobless claims tomorrow are expected to rise slightly, in a WSJ survey. The 10-year gains 0.029 percentage point to 4.315% and the two-year rises 0.055 p.p. to 3.994%. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

1902 GMT - The recent turbulence in financial markets is making corporate bonds look cheaper relative to Treasurys, but still not enough to compensate for the increased risk stemming from policy uncertainty tied to President Trump, Madison Investments' Mike Sanders says. The ICE BofA U.S. High Yield Index Option-Adjusted Spread has widened to 3.2% as of yesterday, from 2.6% in late January, according to FRED data. "We have been reducing some relative credit risk in our portfolios," Sanders says. He is buying more mortgages and owns "a good amount" of Treasurys. "We are just kind of watching the market and seeing if there is an opportunity to add either mortgages or credit." (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

1834 GMT - Royal Bank of Canada reckons there won't be a race to the bottom for Canadian interest rates beyond what it has already expected this year projecting the central bank's policy rate will decline to 2.25% around midyear. That's despite the turmoil the trade war with the U.S. is expected to bring. Economist Claire Fan says that after the latest quarter-point cut to 2.75% data releases will going forward be monitored closed for signs weaker sentiment is reflected in actual spending, GDP and labor numbers. Data also will be watched for any evidence the uptick in inflation in recent months is persisting and pushing up expectations, and for any fiscal response from Ottawa to addressing trade disruptions. (robb.stewart@wsj.com; @RobbMStewart)

data.title: Couche-Tard goes on charm offensive for Japan's Seven & i after board shake-up
data.description:
Key points:
  • Canadian firm to hold first press conference in Tokyo since announcing bid last August
  • Couche-Tard says it is confident it can overcome antitrust concerns
  • The $47 billion deal, if completed, would be Japan's biggest-ever foreign buyout

Alimentation Couche-Tard is seeking to win over the Japanese public for its $47 billion bid for Seven & i on Thursday and address antitrust concerns that have emerged as a major stumbling block in its quest to buy the 7-Eleven owner.

Canada's Couche-Tard, which owns the Circle-K convenience store chain, has been pursuing Seven & i for months even as it has received a frosty reception from the Japanese retail giant, in what would be Japan's largest-ever foreign buyout if the deal is completed.

Executives from Couche-Tard are holding their first press conference in Tokyo on Thursday, months after announcing a bid for Seven & i in August and highlighting the Canadian company's recent push to woo a Japanese public sceptical of a foreign takeover.

The event comes a day after two of Seven & i's independent directors resigned from the board, a development that one shareholder, U.S.-based Artisan Partners said was a "sign of dysfunction" at Seven & i. Artisan has repeatedly called on the Japanese company to engage more actively with Couche-Tard.

Seven & i has repeatedly said that potential antitrust issues in the United States would make the proposed takeover difficult.

Couche-Tard said this week it was confident there was a "clear path" to overcome any U.S. regulatory hurdles and expressed frustration at the 7-Eleven owner's "limited engagement."

Couche-Tard also said it had been working with Seven & i on a plan to divest some of their stores in the United States.

Seven & i's newly appointed CEO Stephen Dacus has reiterated that significant regulatory hurdles stand in the way of a deal. The firms are the top two players in the U.S. convenience store market, with about 20,000 locations between them.

Couche-Tard has offered to pay $18.19 per share in Seven & i, representing a roughly 23% premium over the Japanese company's share price of 2,196 yen ($14.82) on Thursday.

ANTITRUST ISSUES

Couche-Tard management's trip to Tokyo and the engagement with Seven & i on antitrust concerns underscore the lengths to which dealmakers would go to ensure deal certainty amid U.S. regulatory scrutiny.

To engage in detailed divestment discussions for antitrust purposes before a deal is agreed or any confidentiality agreement is signed is uncommon in transactions, deal advisers said.

"I can't say I've seen a case where prior to a merger agreement being executed the entire divestiture package and buyer were set in stone and baked into the merger agreement," said Kathy O'Neill, a partner at law firm Fried Frank.

But she said working on a divestiture package before a merger agreement was reached would help to potentially reduce the risk of surprise and time and effort put into chasing a deal.

Tim Cornell, a litigation partner and member of the Debevoise & Plimpton's Antitrust Group, agreed the airing of antitrust concerns before a deal was announced was not typical.

"In certain circumstances, buyers will test the waters with regards to a divestiture package especially where they've identified that's what is needed," he said.

Couche-Tard sweetened its offer in October and has said it remained committed to the deal, after a competing $58 billion management buyout proposed by Seven & i's founding family failed to materialise.

data.title: China's YTO Express falls as Alibaba's affiliate plans stake cut
data.description:
** Shares of China's courier delivery services group YTO Express Group Co Ltd fall 5% to 13.11 yuan, lowest since March 12, 2024

** Stock set for the biggest one-day decline since October 9, 2024

** Alibaba's Hong Kong shares fall 2.3%

** The blue-chip CSI 300 Index gains 0.2%, the Shanghai Composite Index adds 0.1%

** YTO stock down 6.8% YTD

data.title: Stecon Group Target Price Lowered to THB9.00 From THB11.00 by Thanachart Securities >STECON.TH
Naver Raised to Buy From Neutral by Nomura >035420.SE
India's benchmark indexes may open higher on soft US, domestic data
data.description:
India's benchmark indexes are likely to open higher on Thursday, tracking global market trends, as investors draw comfort from softer-than-expected inflation data both in the U.S. and domestically.

However, the gains are expected to be limited as U.S. President Donald Trump has threatened to escalate the trade war with additional tariffs on Europe and Canada, following their announcement of retaliatory tariffs in response to his previous trade barriers.

The GIFT Nifty futures (GIFc1) were trading at 22,572, as of 07:39 a.m. IST, indicating that the blue-chip Nifty 50 will likely open above Wednesday's close of 22,470.50.

Asian markets were mixed on the day, with MSCI Asia ex-Japan (.MIAPJ0000PUS) up 0.2%.

U.S. consumer prices increased moderately in February, although it is seen as a temporary relief with data not fully capturing the impact of Trump's tariffs.

Higher U.S. tariffs on key trading partners are expected to fuel inflation in the world's largest economy, potentially prompting the Federal Reserve to maintain higher interest rates.

Higher U.S. interest rates are unfavorable for India and other emerging markets, as they make these markets less attractive in comparison with the dollar and Treasuries.

Foreign investors have sold Indian shares worth more than $28 billion since late-September, with 16.28 billion rupees ($186.7 million) on Wednesday as per provisional data.

Investors are also worried that an escalating global trade war could push the U.S. economy into a recession.

Back home, India's retail inflation fell below 4% in February, giving the central bank room to cut rates further in coming meetings.

STOCKS TO WATCH

** Infosys announces expansion of strategic collaboration with U.S.-based financial institution Citizens for artificial intelligence-driven transformation

** BEML and Siemens sign pact to explore train and metro markets

** Bharat Electronics receives 24.63 billion rupees order from the Indian Air Force

($1 = 87.2075 Indian rupees)

data.title: Iron Ore Prices Higher; Recovering Demand Lends Support — Market Talk
data.description: Iron ore prices are higher in the early Asia trade but analysts remain neutral on its prices in the short term. Though the prices have seen some support from recovering demand, they are likely to stay at the current levels for a while, Citic Securities analysts write in a note. Investors should watch for China's macro policies developments given uncertain geopolitical tensions, they add. The most-traded iron-ore contract on the Dalian Commodity Exchange is 0.7% higher at 781.5 yuan a ton.(jiahui.huang@wsj.com; @ivy_jiahuihuang)

data.title: Renascor Resources Posts Flat Earnings in Fiscal H1; Shares Hit Four-Year Low
data.description:
Renascor Resources reported Thursday fiscal half-year earnings of AU$0.0004 per diluted share, unchanged from the year-earlier period.

Total revenue for the six months ended Dec. 31, 2024, was AU$2.7 million, compared with AU$2.4 million a year earlier.

Renascor Resources' shares were down over 2% in recent Thursday trade, earlier hitting their lowest since January 2021.

data.title: Dollar Holds Steady Amid Escalating Trade War
data.description:
The US dollar index remained stable around 103.5 on Thursday after gaining ground in the previous session, as investors weighed the impact of an escalating global trade war on the economy and consumers.

In the latest developments, President Trump vowed to impose additional tariffs, following retaliatory measures from the EU and Canada in response to his steel and aluminum duties.

Trump also reiterated warnings of reciprocal tariffs set to take effect next month, fueling further uncertainty in global markets.

Meanwhile, US consumer inflation data released on Wednesday came in softer than expected for February, offering some relief to investors.

However, the full impact of newly implemented tariffs has yet to be realized, raising concerns that inflation could pick up again in the coming months.

Looking ahead, the Fed is set to announce its policy decision next week, with markets widely expecting the central bank to keep rates unchanged while unveiling updated economic projections.

data.title: Couche-Tard Chairman Bouchard: Value Local Leadership in Transaction With Seven & I
Financial Times - March 13
data.description:
The following are the top stories in the Financial Times. Reuters has not verified these stories and does not vouch for their accuracy.

Headlines

- Intel appoints new chief after abrupt exit of Pat Gelsinger

- Starmer to target 'cottage industry of blockers' in overhaul of regulators

- US regulator backtracks on request to delay Amazon Prime trial

- Harold Hamm's Continental Resources expands into Turkey to develop oil reserves

Overview

- Intel on Wednesday named former board member and chip industry veteran Lip-Bu Tan as its CEO after the troubled U.S. chipmaker's board ousted Pat Gelsinger in December.

- British Prime Minister Keir Starmer will on Thursday vow to reshape public services by taking on a "cottage industry of checkers and blockers" and embracing AI to drive through efficiencies, though Downing Street insisted he would not take "a chainsaw" to the state.

- The U.S. Federal Trade Commission has backtracked on a request to delay the trial in its high-profile case challenging Amazon's Prime service, rejecting its own lawyer's claim of resource constraints.

- Turkish national oil company TPAO has signed a joint venture agreement with U.S. oil producer Continental Resources to develop shale fields in the country's Diyarbakir Basin.

data.title: Tarsus Pharmaceuticals Specializes in Developing Treatments for Eye-Care Diseases >TARS
Gold Approaches Record Highs
data.description:
Gold rose toward $2,940 per ounce on Thursday, approaching record levels, as escalating global trade tensions continued to fuel safe-haven demand.

On Wednesday, President Donald Trump warned of additional tariffs on EU goods after the EU and Canada retaliated against existing US trade barriers.

Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick also said that Trump would impose trade protections on copper as well.

Meanwhile, both headline and core US inflation came in below expectations, reassuring investors about disinflation and giving the Federal Reserve more flexibility for a less restrictive stance.

However, the impact of tariffs has yet to materialize, and inflation could rise again in the coming months.

data.title: Strickland Metals Logs Up to About 89% Gold Recovery at Western Australia Project; Shares Rise 6%
data.description:
Strickland Metals recorded gold recovery of up to about 89% via metallurgical test work of six fresh rock composite samples from the Palomino deposit at the Horse Well gold camp as part of the Yandal project in Western Australia, according to a Thursday filing with the Australian bourse.

This means "high gold recoveries can be expected by conventional processing and treatment," said Strickland managing director, Paul L'Herpiniere. He added that the samples contained no carbon or arsenic and had very low silver, which de-risks future developments.

Strickland Metals' shares were up 6% in recent Thursday trade.

data.title: Couche-Tard Bouchard: Believe There Is Clear Path to Regulatory Approval
Aeris Resources Says Rob Brainsbury Remains on Extended Personal Leave
Aeris Resources Names Dane Van Heerden as Permanent CFO
Aeris Resources Says Brainsbury to Return to New Role
Synagistics Launches Geene TurboGT AI Chatbot
data.description:
Synagistics launched its Geene TurboGT artificial intelligence chatbot, an upgraded version of the Geene AI platform, according to a Thursday filing with the Hong Kong bourse.

Geene TurboGT utilizes low-latency processing, featuring a response time of less than 1 second, faster than ChatGPT 4.5's time of 1 to 3 seconds. It also features what it calls bias-free and transparent answers, and is designed for business use, content creators, researchers, and developers, the filing said.

data.title: Tarsus Expects Offering to Close March 14 >TARS
Tarsus Expects Gross Proceeds of About $125.0M From Offering >TARS
Comet Ridge Says Initial Gas Flow Achieved Ahead of Expectations at Queensland Pilot Production Scheme
data.description:
Comet Ridge said the Mahalo East pilot production scheme in Queensland has been producing natural gas for over two weeks, with initial gas flow achieved ahead of expectations, according to a Wednesday filing with the Australian bourse.

The initial gas flow was achieved within five and a half weeks of dewatering, allowing for expansion of the available area for development within the Mahalo Gas Hub, the filing said.

The production test will continue until the gas rate trend is established enough to predict future performance and book reserves, according to the filing.

Shares rose past 4% in recent Thursday trade, earlier hitting their lowest since September 2021.

** Stock hits lowest since March 6

** Chinese miner plans to sell 630 mln new shares to key shareholder Jinchuan (BVI) 1 Limited at HK$0.628, a ~14% discount to Wednesday's close, to raise HK$395.6 mln (~$51 mln)

** Proceeds will be used to develop copper and cobalt Musonoi Project in Congo's Lualaba Province

** The key shareholder will subscribe for new shares after the same amount of existing shares are sold at the same price to third party investors

** YTD, stock up 23%

($1 = HK$7.7704)

data.title: XRP Faces Bearish MVRV Crossover—Price Plunge To Continue?
data.description:
On-chain data shows the XRP Market Value to Realized Value (MVRV) Ratio has just gone through a crossover that may not be positive for the asset’s price.

XRP MVRV Ratio Has Dropped Under Its 200-Day MA

As pointed out by analyst Ali Martinez in a new post on X, the MVRV Ratio’s latest crossover may signal a macro trend shift for XRP. The “MVRV Ratio” refers to an on-chain indicator that keeps track of the ratio between a given asset’s market cap and its realized cap.

The realized cap is a capitalization model that calculates the cryptocurrency’s ‘true’ value by assuming that the real value of any token in circulation is the spot price at which it was last transacted on the network.

The previous transaction price of any coin can be considered the value at which its investor purchased it, so the realized cap represents the total amount of capital that the asset’s holders as a whole spent to purchase their holdings. In contrast, the market cap just tells us what value the investors are holding in the present.

Since the MVRV Ratio takes the ratio between these two metrics, it basically signifies the profit-loss status of the market as a whole. The indicator being greater than 1 suggests investors are holding more than they invested, while it being under the mark implies a dominance of underwater tokens.

Now, here is the chart shared by the analyst that shows the trend in the XRP MVRV Ratio, as well as its 200-day moving average (MA), over the last few months:

As displayed in the above graph, the XRP MVRV Ratio has witnessed a plunge recently as a result of the drawdown that the cryptocurrency’s price has gone through.

Though, despite the price crash, the indicator’s value continues to be well above the 1 level, meaning that the investors of the asset are still largely in the green. That said, there is one important level that the metric has indeed dipped below due to the downtrend: the 200-day MA.

The MVRV Ratio broke above the level in the last few months of 2024 and remained above it until this breakdown. From the chart, it’s apparent that the previous crossover proved to be a bullish signal for XRP.

Now that the reverse crossover has occurred, the analyst has noted that another potential macro trend shift could be ahead for the cryptocurrency’s price. It only remains to be seen whether the asset will continue to decline in the coming days and confirm the pattern or not.

XRP Price

At the time of writing, XRP is floating around 2.23, up more than 5% over the last seven days.
